mappings.rationaleAI: The Indus Valley Civilisation directly covers all sub-paths of B.2: urban centres at Harappa and Mohenjo-daro (B.2.a), agriculture, metallurgy, pottery, and trade (B.2.b), and language and religious developments (B.2.c). This is the primary article for the Indus civilization section.

Ashoka is the central figure of B.5.a (consolidation of the Mauryan empire) and his dhamma-based governance directly addresses B.5.b (Mauryan economic, social, and administrative developments). His patronage of Buddhism and state-sponsored religious tolerance also relates to B.4.a's early development of Buddhism.

Alexander the Great's invasion of northwestern India in 327 BC is the precise event named in B.4.b, and the establishment of Greek settlements and resulting Hellenistic influence on South Asia (Greco-Buddhism, Indo-Greek kingdoms) is directly relevant to B.6 (rise of small kingdoms including Indo-Greek rulers).

The Gupta Empire (c. 320–540 CE) is the central subject of outline path C.1, covering its expansion, administration, and the Hunnic invasions that ended it. Its cultural efflorescence in mathematics (Aryabhata), literature (Kalidasa), and Hindu consolidation also connects to C and D.3's social, economic, and cultural developments.

The Neolithic Revolution directly covers Late Stone Age to Neolithic settlement patterns, including Baluchistan and the Indus Valley as one of its independent agricultural centers, corresponding to B.1 (Late Stone Age hunters and Neolithic settlement). It provides the broader framework for understanding India's transition to sedentary agriculture.

The Achaemenid Empire controlled the Indus Valley region (its easternmost satrapy) during the Pre-Mauryan period (B.4), providing the political context for Alexander's invasion (B.4.b) and the establishment of Greek settlements. It directly shaped the political landscape of northwestern India outlined in B.4.

Ancient Greece covers Alexander's Indian campaign (B.4.b) and the resulting Hellenistic period that produced the Indo-Greek kingdoms directly named in B.6 (rise of small kingdoms with Indo-Greek rulers). Greek cultural influence on Gandhara art and religion is substantive for this section.

The Stone Age article directly covers the prehistoric period relevant to B.1 (Late Stone Age hunters in Baluchistan and Indus Valley), addressing lithic technology, hunter-gatherer subsistence, and the transition to Neolithic agriculture that preceded Indus Valley civilization.

The Silk Road covers maritime trade networks that connected South India with the West (B.7.a: development of guilds, banking, and extensive maritime trade), and the broader overland and maritime commerce that shaped cultural and economic development in D.3's feudalism and economic decentralization.

History of agriculture covers the Indian subcontinent as one of its independent agricultural origins, relevant to B.1 (Neolithic settlement) and B.2.b (Indus Valley agriculture and animal husbandry). The development of Ganges Valley agriculture through B.3 is also addressed in its survey of South Asian farming history.

Pottery is among humanity's oldest technologies and the Indus Valley Civilisation produced distinctive ceramic traditions directly relevant to B.2.b (developments in metalwork, pottery, and transportation). The article contextualizes South Asian pottery within global ceramic history and archaeological significance.

Sumer had documented trade connections with the Indus Valley, directly relevant to B.2.b's coverage of Indus transportation and trade. The article addresses Sumerian trade networks including connections to the Indus Valley, making it the primary source for contextualizing Indus-Mesopotamia exchange.

Post-classical history covers South and Southeast Asia including Indian kingdoms of the 500–1200 CE period, directly corresponding to sections C (Gupta successors, Deccan kingdoms, Pallava South India) and D (North India 750–1200, Chola rise). It frames Indian developments within the global post-classical system of trade and religious diffusion.

The Himalayas article addresses the physical geography of the Indian subcontinent that shaped its civilizations, directly relevant to outline path A's coverage of the geography and ethnography of the Indian subcontinent. As the primary geographic barrier defining the subcontinent, it is essential context for the introduction.

History of art covers Indian, Buddhist, and Hindu artistic traditions including the Gandhara and Gupta periods, directly relevant to B.7.b (patronage of religious art and literature in South Indian civilizations), C.3 (Pallava developments in religious art and architecture), and D.3 (literature and the visual arts in medieval India).

Ancient history (writing to AD 500) encompasses South Asia as a covered region, providing broad coverage of Indian prehistoric and early historic civilization corresponding to the full B section (prehistoric period to AD 300). It addresses the Indus Valley, Vedic cultures, and the Mauryan empire within the three-age archaeological framework.
